message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The pathophysiology of COVID-19 associated thrombosis seems to be multifactorial, involving interplay between cellular and plasmatic elements of the hemostasis. We hypothesized that COVID-19 is accompanied by platelet apoptosis with subsequent alteration of the coagulation system. We investigated depolarization of mitochondrial inner transmembrane potential (ΔΨm), cytosolic calcium (Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  ) concentration, and phosphatidylserine (PS) externalization by flow cytometry. Platelets from intensive care unit (ICU) COVID-19 patients (n=21) showed higher ΔΨm depolarization, cytosolic Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  concentration and PS externalization, compared to healthy controls (n=18) and COVID-19 non-ICU patients (n=4). Moreover significant higher cytosolic Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  concentration and PS was observed compared to septic ICU control group (ICU control). In ICU control group (n=5; ICU non-COVID-19) cytosolic Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  concentration and PS externalization was comparable to healthy control, with an increase ΔΨm depolarization. Sera from ICU COVID-19 13 patients induced significant increase in apoptosis markers (ΔΨm depolarization, cytosolic Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  concentration and PS externalization). compared to healthy volunteer and septic ICU control. Interestingly, immunoglobulin G (IgG) fractions from COVID-19 patients induced an Fc gamma receptor IIA dependent platelet apoptosis (ΔΨm depolarization, cytosolic Ca
                  <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                  concentration and PS externalization). Enhanced PS externalization in platelets from ICU COVID-19 patients was associated with increased sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) score (r=0.5635) and DDimer (r=0.4473). Most importantly, patients with thrombosis had significantly higher PS externalization compared to those without. The strong correlations between apoptosis markers and increased D-Dimer levels as well as the incidence of thrombosis may indicate that antibody-mediated platelet apoptosis potentially contributes to sustained increased thromboembolic risk in ICU COVID-19 patients.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Severe COVID-19 is associated with increased antibody-mediated platelet apoptosis.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Platelet apoptosis in severe COVID-19 is correlated with D-Dimer and higher incidence of thromboembolisms.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Severe COVID-19 infection is associated with increased antibody-mediated platelet apoptosis

reference.article-title: Hypercoagulation and Antithrombotic Treatment in Coronavirus 2019: A New Challenge
